questions.question: According to research, what percentage of older adults experience imposter syndrome specifically about wisdom-sharing?

options.text: About 20-25% experience imposter syndrome about wisdom-sharing
About 40-45% experience imposter syndrome about wisdom-sharing
About 62% experience imposter syndrome about wisdom-sharing
Nearly all (95%+) experience imposter syndrome about wisdom-sharing

questions.explanation: Research suggests approximately 62% of older adults experience imposter syndrome when considering whether their experience qualifies as 'wisdom worth sharing.' This is part of the broader pattern where 62-70% of people experience imposter syndrome at some point. Paradoxically, it often affects high-achievers and those with genuine expertise more than those with inflated self-assessment (Dunning-Kruger effect).

questions.question: According to the lesson, what is the relationship between confidence and action?

options.text: You must build confidence first, THEN take action
Confidence often follows action rather than preceding it—act despite doubt, then confidence builds through experience
Confidence and action are unrelated—one doesn't affect the other
Only naturally confident people should take action; others should wait

questions.explanation: Research shows confidence often follows action rather than preceding it. The common misconception is 'build confidence, THEN act'—but the reality is 'act despite doubt, THEN confidence follows.' Confidence accumulates through survived attempts, not through waiting to feel ready. This is why the lesson emphasizes starting before you feel ready and taking the smallest possible action to build momentum.

questions.question: What is the Dunning-Kruger effect, and how does it relate to imposter syndrome?

options.text: People with limited competence overestimate ability; people with genuine expertise tend to underestimate—so worrying about being unqualified often indicates you ARE qualified
Everyone accurately assesses their own competence regardless of skill level
Experts always feel confident; beginners always feel inadequate
Imposter syndrome only affects people who are actually frauds

questions.explanation: The Dunning-Kruger effect shows that people with limited competence often overestimate their ability (peak of 'Mount Stupid'), while people with genuine expertise tend to underestimate (valley of 'Imposter Syndrome'). This means if you're worried about being unqualified, you're probably MORE qualified than those who aren't worried. Actual frauds don't tend to self-reflect this deeply—the fact that you're questioning whether you're wise enough is evidence you're wise enough to question it.

questions.question: What are the 4 questions in the Feedback Filter for evaluating criticism?

options.text: Who said it? When did they say it? Where did they say it? Why did they say it?
Is this person in the arena? Is there a kernel of truth? Does it align with my values? Is this pattern or outlier?
Do I like this person? Do they like me? Am I being too sensitive? Should I ignore this?
Is this criticism valid? Should I defend myself? Can I change? Will they apologize?

questions.explanation: The Feedback Filter includes four questions: (1) Is this person in the arena? (doing similar work, taking similar risks, qualified to assess), (2) Is there a kernel of truth? (even harsh criticism can contain useful insight), (3) Does this align with my values and goals? (some criticism reflects different values, not your failure), (4) Is this pattern or outlier? (one person's criticism may be idiosyncratic; multiple people suggesting similar feedback indicates a pattern). These help you integrate legitimate feedback while filtering out unconstructive criticism.

questions.question: How can you distinguish productive hesitation from self-sabotage when deciding whether to share wisdom?

options.text: Productive hesitation involves specific, addressable concerns; self-sabotage involves vague anxiety, perfectionism, or comparison
All hesitation is productive—you should always wait until you feel completely ready
All hesitation is self-sabotage—you should push through any doubt immediately
There's no way to distinguish—hesitation always means you're not ready

questions.explanation: Productive hesitation involves: specific concerns you can name and address, sense of 'not yet' with clear conditions for readiness, feedback suggesting more preparation, ethical concerns, or genuine capacity limits. Self-sabotage involves: vague diffuse anxiety, moving goalposts ('I'll be ready when...' but the when keeps changing), comparison ('Others are better'), perfectionism ('Not good enough yet'), or fear of judgment without real risk of harm. Productive hesitation warrants addressing the specific concern then acting; self-sabotage warrants acknowledging fear then acting despite it.

title: Overcoming Barriers to Sharing Your Wisdom - Quiz
